expanded_description: This culminating section synthesizes all the logical tools from Unit 2—inductive and deductive reasoning, conditional statements, algebraic properties, and formal proof structure—into comprehensive practice. Students apply the full proof-writing process to multi-step problems involving segments, angles, parallel lines, and perpendicular lines, building mastery of the deductive reasoning that defines geometry as a logical system.

core_concept.principle: Geometric proof is the synthesis of all reasoning skills: identifying given information, recognizing applicable definitions and theorems, constructing logical chains, and justifying every step with valid reasons. Unit 2 has built this capacity piece by piece—from conditional statements through parallel and perpendicular line proofs—and this review integrates them into fluent, confident proof-writing.
core_concept.explanation: Think of this section as the championship game after a season of practice. Each previous section taught a specific skill: forming conjectures, analyzing conditionals, applying laws of detachment and syllogism, using algebraic properties, structuring two-column proofs, and proving theorems about segments, angles, parallel lines, and perpendicular lines. Now all these skills come together. A single complex proof might require recognizing a linear pair, applying the Vertical Angles Theorem, using the Corresponding Angles Postulate, substituting using algebraic properties, and concluding with a converse. The ability to fluidly combine these tools is the hallmark of geometric proficiency.

image.alt: A central proof with multiple logical branches, each labeled with a different Unit 2 skill: Conditional Logic, Law of Detachment, Algebraic Properties, Segment/Angle Theorems, Parallel Line Theorems, Perpendicular Theorems. All branches converge on the conclusion. The proof is surrounded by icons representing each skill area.
image.caption: Unit 2 Review: synthesizing all reasoning tools into complete geometric proofs.

sections.introduction: Writing a geometric proof from start to finish involves a systematic process that draws on every skill from Unit 2. This section reviews the complete process and provides a framework for tackling any proof problem, from simple two-step arguments to complex multi-theorem proofs.

introduction_image.alt: A flowchart of the complete proof-writing process: (1) Read and Diagram — draw, label, mark given. (2) Identify Given and Prove. (3) Analyze — what definitions, postulates, theorems apply? (4) Plan — work forward from givens, backward from conclusion. (5) Write — two-column format with justified steps. (6) Verify — check each step's logic and justification.
introduction_image.caption: The complete proof-writing process: a systematic approach from reading the problem to verifying the solution.

key_concept.definition: The proof-writing process integrates all Unit 2 skills: (1) Interpret the problem using conditional logic (identifying hypothesis and conclusion). (2) Apply deductive reasoning (Law of Detachment and Syllogism) to connect givens to conclusions. (3) Use algebraic properties to manipulate equations. (4) Cite definitions, postulates, and theorems as justifications. (5) Structure the argument in two-column format with statements and reasons.
key_concept.context: This integrated process is the culminating skill of Unit 2. Every subsequent geometry unit—triangles, quadrilaterals, circles, and beyond—builds on this foundation of logical proof. The ability to construct a valid proof is what distinguishes geometry from mere shape recognition.

image.alt: A comprehensive proof-writing framework displayed as a reference card. Five stages with key questions and actions for each stage. Examples of common justifications organized by category: Definitions, Postulates, Theorems, Properties.
image.caption: Proof-writing framework: a systematic approach integrating all Unit 2 reasoning skills.

process_flow.description: Identify the conditional structure: what is given (hypothesis) and what must be proved (conclusion). Translate verbal statements into geometric notation.
process_flow.mechanism: Apply conditional logic from Sections 2.1-2.3. The problem is essentially 'If [given], then [prove].' Write the given as a list of facts. Write the prove statement clearly. Mark all given information on the diagram using appropriate symbols (congruence marks, right angle squares, parallel arrows).
process_flow.result: A clearly framed problem with Given and Prove statements and a fully labeled diagram.
applications: 'Given: M is the midpoint of AB' translates to AM = MB on the diagram and in the proof.
'Given: ∠1 and ∠2 are supplementary' means m∠1 + m∠2 = 180° is available as a premise.
'Prove: Lines are parallel' means the conclusion will use one of the four converse statements.

image.alt: A problem statement highlighted with 'Given' and 'Prove' parts extracted. The corresponding diagram with all given information marked: congruence ticks, right angle squares, parallel arrows. Labels connecting the text to the diagram.
image.caption: Step 1: Deconstruct the problem into Given and Prove. Mark everything on the diagram.

process_flow.process: Analyze and Plan the Logical Chain
process_flow.description: Determine which definitions, postulates, theorems, and properties connect the given to the conclusion. Work forward and backward to build the logical path.
process_flow.mechanism: From the given, ask 'What follows immediately?' (Law of Detachment). From the conclusion, ask 'What would I need to prove just before this?' Connect the forward and backward paths. Identify which major theorem or postulate is the centerpiece of the proof. Plan the sequence of statements before writing the formal proof.
process_flow.result: A mental outline or flowchart of the proof's logical structure.
applications: 'Given a midpoint → AM = MB → combine with Segment Addition → AM = AB/2.'
'Given vertical angles → they are congruent → use in larger angle sum equation.'
'Given parallel lines → corresponding angles congruent → chain with vertical angles → prove parallelism of another pair.'

image.alt: A flowchart showing forward reasoning from givens (blue) and backward reasoning from conclusion (green). The two paths meet at intermediate steps. Key theorems and postulates labeled on the connecting arrows.
image.caption: Step 2: Plan the proof by reasoning forward from givens and backward from conclusion.

process_flow.description: Execute the plan in formal two-column format. Each statement must be numbered and each reason must cite a valid justification.
process_flow.mechanism: Start with the given statements (Reason: Given). Add each logical step, one inference at a time. Justify with definitions (e.g., 'Definition of Midpoint'), postulates (e.g., 'Segment Addition Postulate'), theorems (e.g., 'Vertical Angles Theorem'), or properties (e.g., 'Substitution Property'). End with the exact statement to be proved.
applications: Statements column: numbered, each a single logical step.

image.alt: A two-column proof being written. Statements on the left, Reasons on the right. Color-coded: Given (blue), Definition steps (green), Postulate/Theorem steps (orange), Algebraic/Property steps (purple), Conclusion (boxed).
image.caption: Step 3: Write the two-column proof, justifying each statement precisely.

process_flow.description: Review the proof for logical gaps, incorrect justifications, circular reasoning, or missing steps. Ensure the conclusion is fully derived from the givens.
process_flow.mechanism: Read each statement and ask: 'Does this follow from previous statements using the cited reason?' Check that no steps are skipped. Verify that all reasons are from the valid categories (Given, Definition, Postulate, Theorem, Property). Confirm the final statement exactly matches the Prove. Check that no assumptions were made without justification.
applications: 'Does step 4 actually follow from steps 2 and 3 using the Transitive Property?'
'Did I accidentally use the converse of a theorem without proving the hypothesis?'
'Is every angle pair correctly identified (corresponding vs. alternate interior)?'

image.alt: A magnifying glass over a completed two-column proof. Green checkmarks appear on each step as it's verified. A red X appears on a potential error with a correction note. Final box shows 'Proof Verified — All Steps Valid.'
image.caption: Step 4: Verify each step's logic and justification. Correct any errors.

image.alt: Complete proof-writing process diagram: Deconstruct → Plan → Write → Verify. Each stage with key actions and an icon. A sample proof traced through all four stages.
image.caption: The complete proof-writing process: a systematic, repeatable approach to any geometry proof.

sections.section_title: Comprehensive Proof Practice: Segments and Angles
sections.introduction: This section provides practice with proofs that combine segment and angle relationships. Problems draw on the Segment Addition Postulate, Angle Addition Postulate, midpoint and angle bisector definitions, complementary and supplementary angle relationships, and algebraic properties.

introduction_image.alt: Three diagrams: (1) Collinear points with midpoints. (2) Intersecting lines with angle bisectors. (3) Complementary angles with algebraic expressions. Each diagram has given information marked and the prove statement indicated.
introduction_image.caption: Segment and angle proof review: midpoints, bisectors, and angle pairs with algebra.

key_concept.definition: Segment and angle proofs combine geometric definitions with algebraic manipulation. Key patterns include: using the Segment Addition Postulate to set up equations, applying midpoint or bisector definitions to establish equalities, using complementary/supplementary definitions to set up sums, and solving for unknowns using algebraic properties.
key_concept.context: These proofs are the most common type in early geometry. They bridge numeric problem-solving (find the value of x) with formal proof structure (prove that x equals a specific value). Mastery of these patterns prepares students for more abstract proofs with triangles and quadrilaterals.

image.alt: A reference card showing common proof patterns: Midpoint proof pattern, Angle Bisector proof pattern, Complementary/Supplementary proof pattern. Each with a mini two-column proof template showing the standard steps.
image.caption: Standard proof patterns for segments and angles: reusable logical templates.

worked_examples.title: Segment and Angle Proof Practice
worked_examples.problem:
Write complete two-column proofs.

1) Given: AB = 3x + 2, BC = 5x − 6, AC = 7x + 4. B is between A and C. Prove: x = 4.

2) Given: ∠A and ∠B are complementary. m∠A = 2x + 10, m∠B = 3x − 5. Prove: x = 17.

3) Given: M is the midpoint of PQ. PM = 4x − 3, MQ = 2x + 9. Prove: x = 6.

step_by_step.explanation:
Statements | Reasons
1) B is between A and C — Given
2) AB + BC = AC — Segment Addition Postulate
3) AB = 3x+2, BC = 5x−6, AC = 7x+4 — Given
4) (3x+2) + (5x−6) = 7x+4 — Substitution
5) 8x − 4 = 7x + 4 — Simplify
6) x − 4 = 4 — Subtraction Property
7) x = 8 — Addition Property (Wait: 8x−4=7x+4 → 8x−7x=4+4 → x=8)

Statements | Reasons
1) ∠A and ∠B are complementary — Given
2) m∠A + m∠B = 90° — Definition of Complementary Angles
3) m∠A = 2x+10, m∠B = 3x−5 — Given
4) (2x+10) + (3x−5) = 90° — Substitution
5) 5x + 5 = 90° — Simplify
6) 5x = 85° — Subtraction Property
7) x = 17 — Division Property

Statements | Reasons
1) M is the midpoint of PQ — Given
2) PM = MQ — Definition of Midpoint
3) PM = 4x−3, MQ = 2x+9 — Given
4) 4x − 3 = 2x + 9 — Substitution
5) 2x − 3 = 9 — Subtraction Property
6) 2x = 12 — Addition Property
7) x = 6 — Division Property

image.alt: Three complete two-column proofs with diagrams: (1) Collinear points A-B-C with algebraic expressions. (2) Complementary angles with algebra. (3) Segment with midpoint and algebra. Each proof complete with statements and reasons.
image.caption: Segment and angle proofs with algebra: Segment Addition, Complementary definition, and Midpoint definition.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Segment Addition Postulate, Definition of Midpoint, Definition of Complementary Angles, and algebraic properties in two-column proof format.

sections.introduction: Parallel line proofs combine angle relationships with logical chains to prove lines are parallel or to find angle measures. These proofs draw on the Corresponding Angles Postulate, the converses of all four parallel line angle theorems, and the connection to perpendicular lines.

introduction_image.alt: Three parallel line diagrams: (1) Proving lines are parallel using angle information. (2) Using parallel lines to find angle measures. (3) Combining parallel and perpendicular line theorems. Each with given information marked.
introduction_image.caption: Parallel line proof review: proving parallelism, finding angles, and connecting to perpendicular lines.

key_concept.definition: Parallel line proofs have two main types: (1) Proving lines are parallel by establishing congruent corresponding, alternate interior, or alternate exterior angles, or supplementary consecutive interior angles. (2) Using given parallel lines to determine angle measures through the Corresponding Angles Postulate and related theorems.
key_concept.context: These proofs are the culmination of Sections 2.7, 2.8, and 2.9. They require accurate identification of angle pairs and correct application of both forward theorems (parallel → angle relationships) and converse theorems (angle relationships → parallel).

image.alt: A reference card: Left side — Forward direction (Given parallel → angle relationships). Right side — Converse direction (Given angle relationships → prove parallel). Both sides with diagrams and the applicable postulate/theorem names.
image.caption: Parallel line proofs: forward (using parallelism) and converse (proving parallelism).

worked_examples.title: Parallel Line Proof Practice
worked_examples.problem:
Write complete two-column proofs.

1) Given: m∠1 = 75°, m∠5 = 75°. Prove: a ∥ b. (∠1 and ∠5 are corresponding angles.)

2) Given: a ∥ b, m∠2 = 110°. Find and prove m∠6. (∠2 and ∠6 are corresponding angles.)

3) Given: p ⊥ t, q ⊥ t. Prove: p ∥ q.

step_by_step.step: 1) Prove parallel from congruent corresponding angles
step_by_step.explanation:
1) m∠1 = 75° — Given
2) m∠5 = 75° — Given
3) m∠1 = m∠5 — Transitive Property (both equal 75°)
4) ∠1 ≅ ∠5 — Definition of Congruent Angles
5) ∠1 and ∠5 are corresponding angles — Definition
6) a ∥ b — Converse of Corresponding Angles Postulate

1) a ∥ b — Given
2) m∠2 = 110° — Given
3) ∠2 and ∠6 are corresponding angles — Definition
4) ∠2 ≅ ∠6 — Corresponding Angles Postulate
5) m∠2 = m∠6 — Definition of Congruent Angles
6) m∠6 = 110° — Substitution

step_by_step.step: 3) Two lines perpendicular to same line are parallel
step_by_step.explanation:
1) p ⊥ t — Given
2) q ⊥ t — Given
3) m∠1 = 90° — Definition of Perpendicular Lines (p ⊥ t at their intersection)
4) m∠2 = 90° — Definition of Perpendicular Lines (q ⊥ t at their intersection)
5) m∠1 = m∠2 — Transitive Property
6) ∠1 ≅ ∠2 — Definition of Congruent Angles
7) ∠1 and ∠2 are corresponding angles — Definition
8) p ∥ q — Converse of Corresponding Angles Postulate

image.alt: Three complete proofs with diagrams: (1) Corresponding angles congruent → parallel. (2) Given parallel → corresponding angles → find measure. (3) Perpendicular to same line → parallel. Each with full two-column format.
image.caption: Parallel line proofs: proving parallelism, using parallelism, and connecting perpendicular to parallel.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) a ∥ b (Converse of Corresponding Angles Postulate).
2) m∠6 = 110° (Corresponding Angles Postulate + Substitution).
3) p ∥ q (two lines perpendicular to the same line are parallel).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Corresponding Angles Postulate and its converse, Definition of Perpendicular, and Transitive Property in parallel line proofs.

sections.introduction: The most challenging proofs combine multiple concepts—segments, angles, parallel lines, perpendicular lines, and algebra—in a single logical chain. These mixed proofs develop the strategic thinking needed for advanced geometry.

introduction_image.alt: A complex diagram with multiple elements: intersecting lines, a transversal, marked angles, labeled segments. The given information draws from several Unit 2 topics. The proof requires combining segment, angle, parallel, and perpendicular reasoning.
introduction_image.caption: Mixed proofs: combining multiple geometric concepts in a single logical argument.

key_concept.definition: Mixed proofs require the strategic selection of tools from across Unit 2. The proof-writer must recognize which definitions apply, which theorems connect the given information, and which converses lead to the desired conclusion. There is often more than one valid proof path—choosing the most efficient path is a mark of proficiency.
key_concept.context: These proofs mirror the complexity of real geometric reasoning. In later units, proofs about triangles, quadrilaterals, and circles routinely combine angle relationships, segment relationships, parallel line properties, and algebraic manipulation.

image.alt: A decision tree for complex proofs: 'What is given?' branches to 'Segments → use Segment Addition/Midpoint,' 'Angles → use Angle Addition/Bisector/Pairs,' 'Parallel lines → use Corresponding Angles/Converses,' 'Perpendicular → use Right Angle theorems.' Each branch leads to further choices and eventual conclusion.
image.caption: Mixed proof strategy: classify the given information, select appropriate theorems, and build the logical chain.

worked_examples.title: Mixed Proof Practice
worked_examples.problem:
Write complete two-column proofs for these mixed-application problems.

1) Given: AB ∥ CD, EF ⊥ AB. Prove: EF ⊥ CD.

2) Given: ∠1 ≅ ∠2 (alternate interior angles relative to lines m and n with transversal t). Prove: m ∥ n. Then, given also that m∠1 = 4x − 10 and m∠2 = 2x + 30, find and prove x = 20.

3) Given: M is the midpoint of AB, N is the midpoint of CD, AB ∥ CD. Prove: AM = CN (assuming additional given that AB = CD).

step_by_step.explanation:
1) AB ∥ CD — Given
2) EF ⊥ AB — Given
3) EF ⊥ CD — Perpendicular Transversal Theorem

step_by_step.step: 2) Prove parallel, then solve for x
step_by_step.explanation:
Proof of parallelism:
1) ∠1 ≅ ∠2 — Given
2) ∠1 and ∠2 are alternate interior angles — Definition
3) m ∥ n — Converse of Alternate Interior Angles Theorem

Proof of x = 20 (using the now-established parallelism):
4) m ∥ n — Proved in step 3
5) ∠1 ≅ ∠2 — Alternate Interior Angles Theorem (forward direction)
6) m∠1 = m∠2 — Definition of Congruent Angles
7) m∠1 = 4x−10, m∠2 = 2x+30 — Given
8) 4x−10 = 2x+30 — Substitution
9) 2x = 40 — Subtraction/Addition Properties
10) x = 20 — Division Property

step_by_step.step: 3) Midpoints with parallel lines and equal segments
step_by_step.explanation:
1) M is the midpoint of AB — Given
2) AM = ½AB — Midpoint Theorem (or: AM = MB, AM+MB=AB → 2AM=AB → AM=AB/2)
3) N is the midpoint of CD — Given
4) CN = ½CD — Midpoint Theorem
5) AB = CD — Given
6) ½AB = ½CD — Multiplication Property (multiply both sides by ½)
7) AM = CN — Substitution (from steps 2, 4, and 6)

image.alt: Three complete proofs: (1) Perpendicular Transversal Theorem applied. (2) Two-part proof: first prove parallel using converse, then solve for x using forward theorem. (3) Midpoints with parallel lines and equal segment lengths.
image.caption: Mixed proofs: combining parallel, perpendicular, segment, and algebraic reasoning.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) EF ⊥ CD (Perpendicular Transversal Theorem).
2) m ∥ n (Converse of Alternate Interior Angles Theorem), x = 20 (Alternate Interior Angles Theorem + algebra).
3) AM = CN (Midpoint Theorem + Substitution).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Perpendicular Transversal Theorem, Converse and forward Alternate Interior Angles Theorem, Midpoint Theorem, and algebraic properties in multi-step proofs.

sections.section_title: Unit 2 Review: Key Concepts and Skills
sections.introduction: This section provides a structured review of all major concepts from Unit 2, organized for study and reference. Each concept is summarized with its key points and connections to other topics.

introduction_image.alt: A concept map of Unit 2: Inductive Reasoning → Conjectures/Counterexamples → Conditional Statements → Deductive Reasoning → Properties of Equality → Two-Column Proofs → Parallel Line Theorems → Perpendicular Line Theorems. Arrows show the logical progression of topics.
introduction_image.caption: Unit 2 conceptual flow: from inductive exploration to formal deductive proof.

key_concept.definition: Unit 2 has developed the complete toolkit for geometric reasoning: (1) Inductive reasoning generates conjectures from patterns. (2) Conditional statements express logical relationships. (3) Deductive reasoning (Law of Detachment and Syllogism) draws valid conclusions. (4) Algebraic properties justify equation manipulation. (5) Two-column proofs provide the formal structure. (6) Parallel and perpendicular line theorems provide specific geometric content for proofs.
key_concept.context: This toolkit is the foundation for all subsequent geometry. Every proof in Units 3-12 will use the reasoning skills, proof format, and logical laws developed here. The ability to construct a valid deductive argument is the essential skill of geometry.

image.alt: A comprehensive reference page: left column lists all major topics with key definitions and formulas. Right column shows the corresponding proof patterns and common justifications. Organized for quick review.
image.caption: Unit 2 comprehensive reference: all major concepts, proof patterns, and justifications.

classifications.value: Inductive reasoning (patterns → conjectures) vs. Deductive reasoning (premises → guaranteed conclusions).
characteristics: Inductive: specific examples → pattern → conjecture. Counterexample disproves. Never proves.
Deductive: given premises + logical laws → guaranteed conclusion. Proof establishes certainty.
Law of Detachment: p→q and p → q.
Law of Syllogism: p→q and q→r → p→r.
classifications.behavior: Inductive reasoning discovers; deductive reasoning proves. Geometry requires both: induction to find patterns worth proving, deduction to establish them as theorems.

classifications.value: If-then statements, converse, inverse, contrapositive, and biconditional.
characteristics: Conditional: p → q. False only when p true and q false.
Converse: q → p. NOT equivalent to original.
Biconditional: p ↔ q. Both directions true.
classifications.behavior: Understanding these relationships prevents logical fallacies and enables proof by contrapositive.

classifications.value: Reflexive, Symmetric, Transitive, Substitution, and operation properties.
characteristics: Reflexive: a = a. Symmetric: a = b → b = a. Transitive: a = b and b = c → a = c.
Substitution: Replace a with b if a = b.
classifications.behavior: These properties justify every algebraic step in a geometric proof.

classifications.value: Angle relationships when parallel lines are cut by a transversal, and their converses.
characteristics: Given parallel: Corresponding ≅, Alternate Interior ≅, Alternate Exterior ≅, Consecutive Interior supplementary.
Converses: Any of the above relationships → lines are parallel.
Perpendicular Transversal: ⊥ to one parallel → ⊥ to other.
classifications.behavior: These theorems are among the most frequently used in all of geometry.

image.alt: Complete Unit 2 review page: All four classification categories displayed with key points, diagrams, and examples. Color-coded for quick reference: Reasoning (blue), Conditionals (green), Properties (purple), Parallel/Perpendicular (orange).
image.caption: Unit 2 complete review: reasoning, conditionals, properties, and line theorems.

sections.introduction: This final practice section provides a comprehensive assessment of Unit 2 skills. Problems range from identifying reasoning types to writing complete multi-step two-column proofs. Work through these problems to assess readiness for the unit assessment.

worked_examples.title: Unit 2 Comprehensive Practice
worked_examples.problem:
1) Identify the reasoning type: 'All geometry students study proofs. Maria is a geometry student. Therefore, Maria studies proofs.'

2) Write the converse, inverse, and contrapositive of: 'If two angles are vertical, then they are congruent.' Which are true?

3) Name the property: 'If AB = CD and CD = EF, then AB = EF.'

4) Given: ∠A and ∠B are supplementary, m∠A = 3x + 15, m∠B = 2x. Prove: x = 33.

5) Given: a ∥ b, c ⊥ a. Prove: c ⊥ b.

6) Given: ∠1 ≅ ∠3 (∠1 and ∠3 are alternate interior angles). Prove: Line m ∥ Line n. Then given m∠1 = 5x − 8 and m∠3 = 3x + 12, find and prove x = 10.

step_by_step.explanation: This is Deductive Reasoning — specifically, the Law of Detachment. The conditional is 'If geometry student → studies proofs.' The premise 'Maria is a geometry student' satisfies the hypothesis. The conclusion 'Maria studies proofs' follows by Detachment.

Original: If two angles are vertical, then they are congruent. (TRUE — Vertical Angles Theorem)
Converse: If two angles are congruent, then they are vertical. (FALSE — congruent angles not necessarily vertical)
Inverse: If two angles are NOT vertical, then they are NOT congruent. (FALSE)
Contrapositive: If two angles are NOT congruent, then they are NOT vertical. (TRUE)
AB = CD and CD = EF → AB = EF. This is the Transitive Property of Equality.

1) ∠A and ∠B are supplementary — Given
2) m∠A + m∠B = 180° — Def. of Supplementary
3) m∠A = 3x+15, m∠B = 2x — Given
4) (3x+15) + 2x = 180° — Substitution
5) 5x + 15 = 180° — Simplify
6) 5x = 165° — Subtraction
7) x = 33 — Division

1) a ∥ b — Given
2) c ⊥ a — Given
3) c ⊥ b — Perpendicular Transversal Theorem

Part A — Prove parallel:
1) ∠1 ≅ ∠3 — Given
2) ∠1 and ∠3 are alternate interior angles — Given/Definition
3) m ∥ n — Converse of Alternate Interior Angles Theorem

Part B — Find x:
4) m ∥ n — Proved
5) ∠1 ≅ ∠3 — Alternate Interior Angles Theorem
6) m∠1 = m∠3 — Def. of Congruent Angles
7) 5x−8 = 3x+12 — Substitution
8) 2x = 20 — Subtraction/Addition
9) x = 10 — Division

image.alt: Six panels corresponding to the six practice problems: (1) Detachment diagram. (2) Four conditionals with truth values. (3) Transitive property illustration. (4) Supplementary angles proof. (5) Perpendicular Transversal diagram. (6) Two-part alternate interior angles proof.
image.caption: Unit 2 comprehensive practice: reasoning types, conditionals, properties, and complete proofs.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) Deductive Reasoning (Law of Detachment).
2) Original TRUE, Converse FALSE, Inverse FALSE, Contrapositive TRUE.
3) Transitive Property of Equality.
4) x = 33.
5) c ⊥ b (Perpendicular Transversal Theorem).
6) m ∥ n (Converse of Alternate Interior Angles Theorem), x = 10.
worked_examples.concept_applied: All Unit 2 skills: reasoning types, conditional logic, algebraic properties, and complete two-column proofs for supplementary angles, perpendicular lines, and parallel lines.

summary: Unit 2 has developed the complete toolkit for geometric reasoning and proof. Key areas: 1) Reasoning: Inductive reasoning discovers patterns; deductive reasoning proves conclusions. Law of Detachment (p→q and p → q) and Law of Syllogism (p→q and q→r → p→r) are the valid inference rules. 2) Conditional Logic: Conditional (p→q), converse (q→p), inverse (¬p→¬q), contrapositive (¬q→¬p). Original ≡ contrapositive; converse ≡ inverse. Biconditional (p↔q) when both directions true. 3) Algebraic Properties: Reflexive, Symmetric, Transitive, Substitution, and the operation properties justify every algebraic step in proofs. 4) Two-Column Proofs: The standard format—statements and reasons—organizes logical arguments with complete transparency. Every step must be justified by Given, Definition, Postulate, Theorem, or Property. 5) Parallel Line Theorems: Corresponding ≅, Alternate Interior ≅, Alternate Exterior ≅, Consecutive Interior supplementary. Their converses prove lines parallel. 6) Perpendicular Line Theorems: Perpendicular Transversal Theorem, lines ⊥ to same line are parallel, distance from point to line is the perpendicular segment. 7) Proof Strategy: Deconstruct the problem, plan the logical chain forward and backward, write with precise justifications, and verify each step. This systematic process applies to all geometric proofs in subsequent units.

review_questions_image.alt: Comprehensive Unit 2 concept map with 'Reasoning and Proof' in center. Main branches: Inductive Reasoning, Deductive Reasoning, Conditional Statements, Properties of Equality, Two-Column Proofs, Parallel Line Theorems, Perpendicular Line Theorems. Each branch with sub-topics and key points.
review_questions_image.caption: Unit 2 Complete Review: the full reasoning and proof toolkit for geometry.
